program_outcomes: Matematik, fen bilimleri ve bilgisayar mühendisliği konularında yeterli bilgi birikimi; bu alanlardaki kuramsal ve uygulamalı bilgileri, karmaşık mühendislik problemlerinin çözümünde kullanabilme becerisi.
Bilgisayar mühendisliği alanındaki problemleri tanımlama, formüle etme ve çözme becerisi; bu amaçla uygun analiz ve modelleme yöntemlerini seçme ve uygulama becerisi.
Bilgisayar mühendisliği alanındaki problemleri tanımlama, formüle etme ve çözme becerisi; bu amaçla uygun analiz ve modelleme yöntemlerini seçme ve uygulama becerisi.

Bilgisayar mühendisliği uygulamalarında karşılaşılan karmaşık problemlerin analizi ve çözümü için gerekli olan modern teknik ve araçları seçme ve kullanma becerisi; bilişim teknolojilerini etkin bir şekilde kullanma becerisi.
Bilgisayar mühendisliği alanındaki problemlerin veya disipline özgü araştırma konularının incelenmesi için deney tasarlama, deney yapma, veri toplama, sonuçları analiz etme ve yorumlama becerisi.
Disiplin içi ve çok disiplinli takımlarda etkin biçimde çalışabilme becerisi; bireysel çalışma becerisi.

Sözlü ve yazılı etkin iletişim kurma becerisi; en az bir yabancı dil bilgisi, etkin rapor yazma ve yazılı raporları anlama, tasarım ve üretim raporları hazırlayabilme, etkin sunum yapabilme, açık ve anlaşılır talimat verme ve alma becerisi.
Yaşam boyu öğrenmenin gerekliliği konusunda farkındalık; bilgiye erişebilme, bilim ve teknolojideki gelişmeleri izleme ve kendini sürekli yenileme becerisi.
Etik ilkelerine uygun davranma, mesleki ve etik sorumluluk ve mühendislik uygulamalarında kullanılan standartlar hakkında bilgi.
Proje yönetimi, risk yönetimi ve değişiklik yönetimi gibi, iş hayatındaki uygulamalar hakkında bilgi; girişimcilik, yenilikçilik hakkında farkındalık, sürdürülebilir kalkınma hakkında bilgi.

Bilgisayar mühendisliği uygulamalarının evrensel ve toplumsal boyutlarda sağlık, çevre ve güvenlik üzerindeki etkileri ve çağın mühendislik alanına yansıyan sorunları hakkında bilgi; mühendislik çözümlerinin hukuksal sonuçları konusunda farkındalık.

courses.name: COMPUTER PROGRAMMING I
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to introduce the basic programming language concepts using C Programming Language. At the end of this course, students will not only learn the syntax and semantics of C programming language, but also complete many difficult programming assignments and lab applications.
courses.description: Introduction to Programming Languages and C Programming Language, Sequential Structures, Expressions and Statements, Selective Structure, Repetitive structure, Function definition and call, function prototypes, external variables, storage classes, automatic/static variables, recursion, arrays, pointers, strings, dynamic memory management, structures, unions, bitwise operations, pre-processors and file operations.
learning_outcomes: Acquisition of the basics of a general purpose, imperative programming language constructs; can implement programs using sequential, selective and repetitive structures.
Ability to write C programs that correspond to mathematical expressions
Ability to design and implement functions.
Ability to design and implement user defined types.
Ability to develop applications that work with pointers using dynamic memory allocation.
Ability to design and implement an algorithm in order to solve a specific problem on computers.
Ability to gain primary file processing ability using C programming language.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Programming Languages and C PL. (Principle components of a computer system, computer memory, machine language, assembly language, higher level languages, history of C, compilation of a C program)
Hafta 2: Sequential Structures (A simple sequential program, character set, built-in C data types, variable declarations, constants, arithmetic operators)

Hafta 3: Expressions and Statements (Evaluation of an expression, precedence and associativity rules, parantheses rule, assignment statements, increment and decrement operators, compound assignment operators, nested assignments, basic input/output library functions, type conversions, simple macros)

Hafta 4: Selective Structure (Logical operators, relational operators, conditional expression operator, conditional statements, nested conditional statements, dangling else problem, multiway conditional statements, constant MCS-switch statements)

Hafta 5: Repetitive structure (Pre-test loop structure, post-test loop structure, while statements, do-while loops, for loops, infinite loops, nested loops, loop interruption, null statement, comma operator)
Hafta 6: Functions-1 (Function definition, local variable declarations, return statement, function call, call by value/reference conceptes)
Hafta 7: Functions-2 (Function prototypes, external variables, storage classes, automatic/static variables, recursion)
Hafta 8: Arrays and Pointers-1 (Basics, array declaration, accessing array elements, initialization, passing arrays as function arguments)
Hafta 9: Arrays and Pointers-2 (Basics of pointers, address and dereferencing ops, pointer type declaration, pointer assignment and initialization)

Hafta 10: Arrays and Pointers-3 (Functions and pointers, call by reference, arrays vs pointers, strings and pointers, string library functions, multi-dimensional arrays and pointers, pointer arrays, command-line aguments, dynamic memory management)

Hafta 11: Structures and unions (Structure definition, structure variable declarations, initialization, accessing members, structure assignments, nested structures, pointers to structures, structures and functions)
Hafta 12: Structures and unions-2 (Arrays of structures, self-referencial structures, linked lists, trees, unions)
Hafta 13: Bitwise Operations (Bitwise operators, precedence and associativity rules, left/right shift operators, bit fields)
Hafta 14: Pre-processors and File Operations (Opening/closing files, writing to a file, appending to a file, reading from a file)
resources: Programming in ANSI C, Kumar & Agrawal, West Publishing Co., 1992

courses.name: ATATURK'S PRINCIPLES AND HISTORY OF REVOLUTION I
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide basic formation about the historical conditions which necessitated the Turkish Revolution, the resistance movement led by Mustafa Kemal Pasha which emerged against the occupation of Anatolia and the military, political and diplomatical dimensions of this resistance movement.
courses.description: Purpose of studying the course "Ataturk's Principles and History of Turkish Revolution" and the term of revolution; synopsis of the reasons that caused the fall of the Ottoman Empire and the emergence of Turkish revolution; disintegration of the Ottoman Empire; Mudros Armistice; condition of the country against occupations and reaction of Mustafa Kemal Pasha; Mustafa Kemal Pasha's arrival to Samsun; being organized through Congresses; the National Forces and the National Pact; opening of the Parliament (Turkish Grand National Assembly) and establishment of Regular Army; National Struggle until the Battle of Sakarya; Battle of Sakarya and the Great Offensive; National Struggle in the fields of education and culture; National Struggle in social and economic fields; from Mudanya to Lausanne.
learning_outcomes: Students explain concepts like reform, revolution and learn the reasons and consequences of the reforms performed in the period of Ottoman Empire.
Students understand the causes of the decline of the Ottoman Empire and make evalutions in the frame of the developments in the West.
Students make comparisons between the innovations in the last century of Ottoman Empire and the revolution period with Atatürk.
Students learn the ideas came to the fore in the last years of the Ottoman Empire and make comparisons between these ideas
Students understand the causes and the consequences of Tripoli, Balkan Wars and the First World War symbolizing the losses in the last century of the Ottoman Empire.
Students learn the aggravated provisions of Mudros and understand how they give shape to the world at the Paris Peace Conference with the World War I.
Students have information about the government and Anatolian people against the occupations and make comparisons in this regard.
Students understand Mustafa Kemal Pasha and his activities ranging from Samsun to Ankara during the National Liberation War.
Students review "Misakı Milli (National Pact)" and make evalutions about why it is an essential document.
Students understand and express the meaning of both democracy and the opening of a new Parliament.
Students compare the National Pact with the Treaty of Sevres and compare the attitute against the Treaty of Sevres between the government in İstanbul and the New Parliament.
Students evaluate the conditions of Mudanya Armistice and Lausanne Peace Treaty and understand the meaning and value of independence.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Political and Social Structure in the Ottoman Empire (the rulers, "the military"-ruled "Reaya"); Classic of Change and Decline of the Ottoman Order of the French Revolution and the Effect of the Ottoman Empire

Hafta 1: April 23, 1920 Opening of the First Parliament (Structure and Functioning of Parliament-Reactions to Parliament) before opening of Parliament, the rebellions after the opening of Parliament; Measures Taken by the Parliament); Sèvres Peace Treaty (the Treaty of Sevres and the Importance of Process-Sevres Treaty.)
Hafta 2: XIX. Century Ottoman Modernization (1789-1807 Selim III- Mahmut II 1808-1839); Tanzimat Era; First Constitutional Era)
Hafta 3: League of the Ittihad-i Osmani Union and Progress (Ittihad-i Osmani II Society and the Young Turks. Constitution Announcement); Second Constitutional Era, March 31 Event
Hafta 4: Reforms and Political Life (Second Constitutional Era, Intellectual Movements); Tripoli War, the Balkan Wars
Hafta 5: The First World War and the Ottoman Empire and the outbreak of the war Causes of World War I the Ottoman Empire in terms of the First World War: Results of the First World War
Hafta 6: The Armistice of Mudros October 30, 1918 (Armistice Signing-Main provisions of the Armistice) and the occupation of the Implementation of the armistice of Mudros
Hafta 7: Political Developments and the Ottoman government during the War (Political Events-After the Armistice, the Governments of the armistice, Izmir-Istanbul, the Press during Invasion)

Hafta 8: January 18, 1919 Paris Peace Conference (the Conference-Conference at a Glance Decisions taken in respect of the Turkish territory) Occupation of Izmir, May 15, 1919; Armistice Environment Societies (National Societies-National Forces-National Asset enemy Societies)
Hafta 9: During the War, Mustafa Kemal Pasha (Anatolia, Mustafa Kemal Pasha Mustafa Kemal Pasha, the pass-IX. Appointment of the Army Inspectorate-Mustafa Kemal landed at Samsun May 19, 1919)

Hafta 10: Circulars and Congresses Period (Havza-Amasya-Erzurum- Sivas Congress and other Congresses); Representative Committee and Istanbul, The Governments of Amasya, Interview of Mustafa Kemal Pasha and the Representative Committee's Occurrence in Ankara
Hafta 12: Establishment of Regular Army, Eastern Front (Armenian Problem-Armenian Stopping Attacks Peace and Results-Gyumri); South Front (Adana - Gaziantep-Urfa, Maras-Front)

Hafta 13: The Western Front (First Battle of Inönü-1921 Fundamental Law on the Organization-I Şubat January 20, 1921-London Conference 21 - 12 March 1921-Adoption of the National Anthem-Soviet Relations and the Moscow Treaty of March 12, 1921 March 16, 1921-II. İnönü The Battle of March 31- 1 April 1921 - Kütahya and Eskişehir Wars 10-24 Sakarya War July 1921-August 23 to September 13 1921-Large Attack and Its Consequences

Hafta 14: Mudanya, the Lausanne Peace Conference (Preparations for the conference - Interruption of the negotiations and the Empire and the First Period-conference Important Events in Turkey and the Treaty-Conference Second Period -An Evaluation of-the signing of the Lausanne Peace Treaty of Lausanne Important Terms of the Treaty of Lausanne, Brings.

courses.name: CALCULUS I
courses.purpose: This lecture deals with limit, continuity, derivative, application of derivative, curve sketching and indefinite integral of univariate functions.
courses.description: Function, limit, continuity, derivative, application of the derivative, curve sketching, differential, linear approximation, indefinite integral.
learning_outcomes: Recognizes basic elementary functions
Knows the definitions of limit, continuity and derivative and evaluates limits
Obtains the derivatives of elementary functions. Finds the derivative of the composition of functions
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Definition of function. Basic elementary functions. Trigonometric functions.
Hafta 2: Definition of function. Basic elementary functions. Trigonometric functions.
Hafta 3: Inverse Trigonometric functions, Exponential and Logarithmic functions.
Hafta 4: Hyperbolic and Inverse Hyperbolic Functions. Definition of the limit, one sided limits.
Hafta 5: Basic trigonometric limits. The concept of continuity.
Hafta 6: Properties of continuous functions on the closed interval-Intermediate value theorem, Bolzano's theorem, local and absolute maxima and minima.
Hafta 7: The Derivative. Basic differentiation rules. Derivative of inverse function. Derivative of trigonometric and inverse trigonometric functions.
Hafta 8: Derivatives of Logaritmic and exponential functios. Logarithmic differentiation. Derivatives of Hypherbolic functions and their inverses. Implicit differentiation.
Hafta 9: Higher derivatives. Increasing, decreasing function. First and second derivative tests for local extrema.
Hafta 10: Applied maximum-minimum problems. Mean value theorem, Rolle's Theorem
Hafta 11: Concavity, inflection point. Indeterminate forms (L’Hospital's rule)
Hafta 13: Differential and linear approximation. Antiderivative. Basic properties of integrals. Methods of integrations (Integration by substitution, integration by parts)
Hafta 14: Integration of irrational functions. Rationalizing substitutions.
resources: Edwards&Penney, Calculus and Analytic Geometry
George B. Thomas, Jr., Thomas' Calculus 13th Edition, 2013, Pearson

courses.name: COMPUTER PROGRAMMING II
courses.purpose: The objective of this course is to focus on language features supporting data abstraction, object oriented programming and generic programming through C++ programming language. In the first part of this course students study the subset of C++ that supports the styles of programming traditionally done in C. It covers fundamental types, expressions and control structures for C++. Modularity is discussed, as supported by namespaces and source files. In the second part, C++ facilities for defining and using new types are introduced. Concrete and abstract types (classes), together with operator overloading, polymorphism and the use of class hierarchies. Then, templates are teached for defining families of types and functions. Moreover, students also work on exception handling, standard library and input/output mechanisms supported by C++.
courses.description: Introduction to C++; Types and Declerations, Expressions & Statements; Pointers, Arrays and Structures; Functions; Classes; Classes, Operator Overloading (Copying objects, assignment vs copy constructors, class objects as members, operator functions); Classes, Operator Overloading (binary and unary operators, operators and user defined types, operators in namespaces, friend functions/classes); Derived Classes (has a/is a relation, inheritance types, class hierachies); Derived Classes (virtual functions, abstract classes, polymorphism); Class Hierarchies (Multiple inheritance, ambiguity resolution, replicated base classes); Class Hierarchies (Virtual base classes, multiple inheritance and access control, run-time type information); Templates (Function templates, instantiation, template classes); Exception Handling (assert library, try/catch expressions); STL Library (container classes: vectors, list, set, map, multiset, multimap, STL algorithms, iterators).
learning_outcomes: Acquisition of the basics of the object oriented and generic programming constructs.
Matematiksel ifadeleri bilgisayarda çözümleyecek C++ programlama kodunu yazar.
Bilgisayar programlama dillerini kavrar.
Ability to design and implement user defined types (classes).
Ability to design and implement object oriented solutions to a varied range of problems on computers.
Ability to design and implement generic libraries using class/function templates for a varied range of domains.
Ability to use STL container classes and algortihms in complex projects.
Ability to write, compile and debug C++ programmes to solve varied range of problems in different domains.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to C++ (A very broad overview of the language constructs and types of programming styles supported by C++ PL)
Hafta 2: Types and Declerations, Expressions & Statements
Hafta 4: Functions (Definition vs. decleration, inline functions, static variables, argument passing, function overloading, scope, default arguments, function pointers)
Hafta 5: Classes ( Structure overview, constructors, static members, constant member functions, self reference, in-class function definition, default constructors, destructors)
Hafta 6: Classes, Operator Overloading (Copying objects, assignment vs copy constructors, class objects as members, operator functions)
Hafta 7: Classes, Operator Overloading (binary and unary operators, operators and user defined types, operators in namespaces, friend functions/classes)
Hafta 8: Derived Classes (has a/is a relation, inheritance types, class hierachies)
Hafta 9: Derived Classes (virtual functions, abstract classes, polymorphism)
Hafta 10: Class Hierarchies (Multiple inheritance, ambiguity resolution, replicated base classes)
Hafta 11: Class Hierarchies (Virtual base classes, multiple inheritance and access control, run-time type information)
Hafta 12: Templates (Function templates, instantiation, template classes)
Hafta 13: Exception Handling (assert library, try/catch expressions)
Hafta 14: STL Library (container classes: vectors, list, set, map, multiset, multimap, STL algorithms, iterators)

courses.name: ATATURK'S PRINCIPLES AND HISTORY OF REVOLUTION II
courses.purpose: This course aims to familiarize the young generation with the political, cultural, and economic dimensions of the Atatürk period and the period after the Turkish Revolution, as well as the ideology of the regime and the historical consciousness of the Republic.
courses.description: The course covers the origins and history of the Turkish Revolution, studies on the Republican regime, Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's domestic and foreign policies, the transition from single-party rule to multi-party political life and its results, Turkey's geopolitical position, the psychological threat to youth, the definition and importance of Kemalism, the formation of the Kemalist Thought System and its basic characteristics, Atatürk's ideas and life, Atatürk and the economy, secularism, and religion.
learning_outcomes: Learn political events of the Atatürk period and gain knowledge about political strife in the early years of the Republic.
Learn the difference between the Sultanate and Caliphate institutions and evaluate the process of removal of these institutions.
Understand that the main purpose of Atatürk was to establish a democratic republic and learn the attempt at multi-party political life in this direction.
Understand the basic principles of Atatürk's foreign policy and evaluate the historical development of Turkey's relations with western and eastern countries.
Learn the six principles of Atatürk's Republic of Turkey and understand why these six principles are important and indispensable.
Understand how World War II was a major disaster for humanity and Turkey's efforts to avoid entering the war.
Understand the 1946 and 1950 elections and the electoral system in terms of their results and their place in Turkish democracy.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Have information about political events of Atatürk Period and have knowledge about political strife in the early years of the Republic.
Hafta 2: Learn the difference between the Sultanate and Caliphate institutions and evaluate the process of removal of these institutions.
Hafta 3: Progressive Republican Party, and Sheikh Said Rebellion
Hafta 4: Free Republican Party and Menemen Incident; Inonu Ataturk-Separation

Hafta 5: An Overview of the Revolution and Target; Reforms carried out in law (Brief Assessment of the Ottoman legal system); Fundamental Organization Act of 1924; Adoption of the Turkish Civil Code; Recognition of Other Basic Law; Women's rights

Hafta 6: Revolution on Education and Culture; A Look at Early Childhood Education System of the Republic; Radically Changing the Education System: Unification of Law; Adoption of the new Turkish alphabet; New History and Understanding of Language; Darulfunun from the University of Istanbul; Fine Arts
Hafta 7: Developments in the economic field; Late Economy; Turkey Economy Congress and results; Economic Activity of the Republic of the First Year; Transition to Practice Statism

Hafta 8: The Revolution made in the social life (Clothing and Finery me Modernization: Law on the hat worn; Tekke, Closing of convents and shrines, the International Clock, Calendar, Numbers, dimension and Week holiday Acceptance of, Surname Law on the Adoption; Developments in the Field of Health)

Hafta 9: After the Lausanne Peace Turkey 1923-1930 (Population Exchange in Greek-Turkish Relations (Etabli problem of the Turkey-Iraq Determination Limit (Mosul Problem), Turkish-French relations, Turkish-Italian relations, Turkish -Sovyet Relations, Relations with Eastern States)

Hafta 10: Second World War Round and Turkish Foreign Policy 1931-1939 (the League of Nations Introduction of Turkey, the Turkish-German Relations, Balkan Pact, Turkish-British relations, Montreux (Montreux) Straits Agreement; Sadabat Pact Hatay Issue)

courses.name: PHYSICS II
courses.purpose: Be able to know the basic laws of electricity and magnetism and to apply those laws for solving problems. To be able to use his/her knowledge in the fields of other sciences and/or engineering. Understanding how physics approach and solve problems in electricity and magnetism.

courses.name: PHYSICS LABORATORY II
courses.purpose: To instruct students of the fundamental laws of physics and the application of scientific data, concepts, and models for use in the natural sciences and real world situations. To provide students with problem solving skills by an approach that describes physical phenomena with relevant mathematical models and formula. To instruct students in the competent use of laboratory equipment to collect and record data, apply relevant mathematical models and perform required computations, and present the derived results as an application of a measured observation of the physical world. To have students prepare a written laboratory report that effectively interprets and communicates their results.
courses.description: The lab course consists of mechanics and electricity experiments. In the mechanics experiments, the use of vernier caliper and micrometer, using force table to experimentally determine the force which balances two other forces, studying and analyzing the position and velocity of the motion with constant velocity, the acceleration of a straight-line motion with constant acceleration, horizontal projectile (two-dimensional) motion of an object moving on an inclined air table, conservation of linear momentum are included. In the electricity experiments, the use of circuit board, resistors and wires to create series and parallel circuits, the use of digital ammeter to measure DC voltage and current, constructing different circuits using resistors, tungsten filament bulb, diode and wires on a breadboard from circuit diagrams are included. The use of oscilloscope commonly used to display and analyze the waveform of electronic signals is also introduced.
learning_outcomes: Students experimentally test theoretical physics statements.
Gains the ability to use laboratory instruments.
Gains knowledge about measurement systems.
Gains experience in group work.
Evaluates the experimentally obtained data and document findings in a report format.
weekly_topics: Hafta 3: Straight Line Motion with Constant Velocity
Hafta 4: Straight Line Motion with Constant Acceleration
Hafta 8: Ohm's law, equivalent resistance for resistors connected in series
Hafta 9: Equivalent resistance for resistors connected in parallel
resources: Physics Laboratory Manual (Ankara University)
Raymond A. Serway, John W. Jewett, Physics for Scientists and Engineers, Ninth Edition
Roger A. Freedman , Hugh D. Young, University Physics with Modern Physics, Pearson Education, 15. Edition

courses.name: NANOSTRUCTURED MATERIALS
courses.purpose: To enable the students understand the basic concepts of nanotechnology, enhance their knowledge of nanomaterials, familiarize them with the properties and applications of nanomaterials, and expose them to nanostructured devices and their applications.
courses.description: Nano and micro electromechanical machines (NEM and MEM) are manufactured in the billions annually for sensing, ink jet printing, automotive applications, communications, and medicine. In medicine, bio MEMS promise to revolutionize biotechnology and biomedical engineering through fabrication of devices under 100 micrometers using novel micro and nano-fabrication techniques. Nanofabrication is the group of techniques that allows scientists and engineers to build structures and devices at the atomic scale. Traditional top-down nanofabrication consists of carving nano scale materials from a bulk structure through chemical means or by using beams of electrons or ions to strip away layers of material. Bottom-up methods create structures by adding atomic layers one at a time by deposition or by molecular or nanoparticle self-assembly.
learning_outcomes: Student will be able to use nanomaterials for various industrial applications
Student will be able to design MEMS / NEMS devices for various applications
The student will have detailed information about nanotechnology and nanomaterials.
resources: Bharat Bhushan (2007), Hand book of Nanotechnology, Springer Hand Book. ISBN: 978-3-540-29855-7.
Jin Zhang, Zhong-lin Wang, Jun Liu, Shaowei Chen and Gang-yu Liu, (2003), Self Assembled Nanostructures, Kluwer Academic/Plenum Publishers.ISBN: 978-0-306-47299-2.

courses.name: ANATOLIA’S PROLOGUE. KÜLTEPE KANESH KARUM AND THE BEGINNINGS OF HISTORICAL AGES IN ANATOLIA
MEDIA LITERACY
courses.purpose: Today, meaning of “literacy” as a requirement to be a part of a community has been gone beyond its meaning from merely an ability to read and write to multiple abilities such as computer literacy and financial literacy. This concerns the raise of media literacy under the scope of multiple literacies. Media literacy refers to becoming conscious, reactive and productive about media texts and thus empower the individuals. Media Literacy is run as an elective course at the schools in Turkey. However, there is no programme at Higher Education Institutions specifically aiming at training pre-service teachers. On this point, the purpose of the course is firstly to teach media literacy knowledge and skills to pre-service teachers and then to train them pedagogically so that they can teach Media Literacy lesson at the schools.
courses.description: Teaching basic contents about media literacy; basics of cyber law; examining related Turkish institutions’ regulations and web sites such as RTÜK, TİB and also English web sites such as Think as You Know; media literacy in Turkey and in the World, parents’ media literacy skills; values, moral developments and internet; Media Literacy course in Turkish schools (7th Grade); research results about Media Literacy course in Turkey; experience, opinions and current situations of teachers and students in Turkey; developing materials and lesson plans for pre-service teachers to teach the course at the schools; creating a community of practice to share knowledge and experience about Media Literacy course.
learning_outcomes: Gaining media literacy skills
Gaining visual interpretation
Able to demonstrate media management skills
weekly_topics: Hafta 3: Examining instituions with regard to media literacy in Turkey
Hafta 4: Media literacy in World and in Turkey
Hafta 6: Serious games, violence and cyberbullying
Hafta 8: Values, moral development and the internet
resources: Potter, W. J. (2016). Introduction to Media Literacy, SAGE publication

courses.name: JUDICIAL PROTECTION OF HUMAN RIGHTS
courses.purpose: The main objectives of the course are to promote and provide information about the basic concepts of constitutional law and human rights and to give the ability of evaluating these concepts and the relevant information. Within the scope of the course, it is aimed to examine the historical emergence of the constitutional law and its intellectual foundations within the framework of comparative examples. It is also aimed to obtain information about the judicial review and protection methods of constitutional rights across different jurisdictions through a comparative approach.
courses.description: In the course, knowledge of the superior law, the idea of constitutionalism, in addition to the philosophical foundations and modern pillars of constitutional law, the concept of the constitution, types of constitutions, human rights, fundamental rights and freedoms, governmental systems, the concept of judicial review and its various forms of appearance and practices in different countries are studied. In this context, decisions of the Constitutional Courts and/or High Courts of several countries on similar constitutional issues are examined from a comparative perspective.
learning_outcomes: Students who take this course will have knowledge of the historical and intellectual development of the constitutional law and basic concepts and institutions such as constitution, constitutionalism, types of constitutions, state types, government systems, human rights, fundamental rights and freedoms and judicial review.
Students who take this course will obtain information on human rights and different judicial protection systems of constitutional rights in the world.

Students who take this course will develop the ability to use the methods of interpretation, will be able to make comparative studies regarding various judicial decisions and analyze the controversial issues of judicial review through the constitutional philosophy.

Recalling the basic concepts and information in the field of constitutional law, the students who take this course will be able to conduct researches on information deficiencies and develop a positive attitude towards lifelong learning.

courses.name: TURKISH-ARMENIAN RELATIONS
courses.purpose: Understanding the role of globalization in history education and obtaining information about the role of globalization in Turkey's relations with other countries. In addition, it is aimed to perceive the role of Turkey in the international system and to develop the ability to interpret the effects of geopolitical developments on Turkey in a cause-effect relationship.
courses.description: First of all, it is necessary to talk about what this course is about and how it will be taught. The content of this course covers the current developments in the recent period of Turkey in general. In this context, what globalization is and the effects of globalization in the region and the world will be discussed. Then, the effects of globalization in history education will be evaluated. The course will also focus on what geopolitics is and the geopolitical importance of Turkey. The course will also focus on the importance of geopolitical structure in Turkey's relations with its neighbors and relations with other states. The lectures will basically consist of a presentation that summarizes the topic of the week, followed by your comments, discussion of critical issues and answering your questions together. In this way, it is aimed that you also actively participate in the lesson.
learning_outcomes: Understands the emergence process of the Armenian issue.

courses.name: Gothic Literature
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to enable students to explore the origins, evolution, and defining characteristics of Gothic literature, understand its cultural and historical significance, analyze its recurring themes and motifs, and examine how the genre has adapted and persisted over time. Students will investigate the reasons behind its enduring appeal and relevance, focusing on how Gothic literature continually engages with universal human fears, societal anxieties, and cultural transformations, allowing it to remain contemporary across different historical periods. Through critical analysis of key texts, students will gain insight into the dynamic interplay between the Gothic and the changing world.
courses.description: This course explores the origins, evolution, and defining characteristics of Gothic literature, focusing on themes such as the sublime, the uncanny, and the grotesque. Students will analyze Gothic tropes, supernatural elements, and iconic spaces such as haunted houses and decaying castles, as well as recurring motifs like doppelgängers. The course also examines the role of the Female Gothic and its contribution to the genre’s development. Through critical readings of key texts, students will investigate how Gothic literature addresses universal human fears, societal anxieties, and cultural transformations, ensuring its relevance across historical periods. By applying various critical approaches, students will gain a deeper understanding of the Gothic’s dynamic interplay with cultural and literary change.
learning_outcomes: Students identify the origins and fundamental characteristics of Gothic literature within a historical context.
Students analyze themes such as the sublime, uncanny, and grotesque employed in Gothic works with a critical approach.
Students question the representational forms of Gothic literature in different periods and their connections to social crises.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: An introduction to the course: A presentation on the origins, historical context, and defining characteristics of Gothic fiction The Birth of Gothic Novel: The Castle of Otranto by Horace Walpole Women's Gothic fiction: Selected Chapters from "The Mysteries of Udolpho" by Ann Radcliffe Men's Gothic Fiction and Transgression: Selected passages from The Monk by Matthew Lewis Romanticism and Gothic: Thematic Sections from Frankenstein by Mary Shelley Gothic Monster Representations and Science: Thematic analysis of Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein American Gothic Literature: Selected Passages from The Fall of the House of Usher by Edgar Allan Poe Midterm Exam Victorian Gothic I: Selected Chapters from Jane Eyre by Charlotte Brontë Victorian Gothic II: Selected Chapters from Dracula by Bram Stoker The Doppelgänger in Gothic Fiction: Selected Chapters from Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson Psychological Gothic: The Yellow Wallpaper by Charlotte Perkins Gilman Modern Gothic: Selected Chapt

courses.name: ENVIRONMENTAL EDUCATION AND SUSTAINABILITY
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to raise environmental awareness in students, to introduce the concept of sustainable development and to develop sensitivity to environmental problems. In addition, it is aimed for students to adopt a sustainable lifestyle with an awareness of individual and social responsibility. In this course, evaluation of individuals' prior knowledge about the natural environment and sustainability will be done. The natural environment and sustainability issues will be examined. How the environment and sustainability issues are handled in different disciplines will be researched in national and international publications. How the concepts related to the environment and sustainability can be added to the educational programs in each branch will be reviewed. Environmental education models integrated into the programs that are widespread in Turkey and some countries will be examined and compared. Measurement tools to determine the knowledge, awareness, attitude, skill and participation levels of individuals regarding the environment will be examined and the use of these tools will be learned. A research on environmental education and sustainability issues will be developed and evaluated by peers.
courses.description: The concept of environment, environmental problems and the causes of these problems, Sustainable development and sustainability principles, Climate change, biodiversity loss, water and air pollution, Energy sources and sustainable energy use, Waste management and recycling, Ecological footprint and carbon footprint, Importance, principles and methods of environmental education, Local and global environmental policies, Ecosystem services and nature-based solutions, Gender and environment relationship, Sustainable cities and living spaces, Increasing environmental awareness in education, Sustainable living practices at individual and social levels, Current practices and projects in the context of environment and sustainability will be examined.
learning_outcomes: Defines the concepts of environment and sustainability.
Analyzes global and local environmental problems.
Explains the basic principles of sustainable development.

courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of human behavior within organizations. It introduces key concepts, theories, and applications of organizational behavior at individual, group, and organizational levels. Students will develop analytical skills to understand organizational dynamics and apply behavioral science principles to real-life organizational issues.
courses.description: This course covers fundamental concepts, theories, and practices aimed at understanding individual and group behavior within organizations. It focuses on key areas of organizational behavior, including individual differences (such as personality, attitudes, perception, and motivation), group dynamics (such as teamwork, communication, and leadership), and organizational structure (such as organizational culture, change, and conflict management). Students will engage in case studies and practical applications to develop the ability to analyze organizational problems and design effective management strategies. The course aims to enable students to approach behavioral issues in the workplace from a scientific and analytical perspective.

courses.name: FUNDAMENTALS OF COSMOLOGY
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to give the basic information on cosmology to the students who do not study in Astronomy and Space Sciences or related departments. It is aimed to enable the student to see the place of the Earth in the universe from a wider perspective and from various historical and scientific perspectives. It is also aimed to give answers to fundamental questions such as what the universe consists of, how its shape/beginning/end, how it changes over time, and how its age can be determined.
courses.description: Description of cosmological distances and types of radiation in the universe. Depictions of the Universe by ancient thinkers in history. Celestial bodies that make up the universe, types of the matter and energy. The main features and structure of the Milky Way Galaxy in which the Solar System is located. Properties of dark matter. General features and types of galaxies and galaxy clusters in the universe. Universe on a large scale. The expansion of the universe. Studying the concepts of space and time together. The first scientific models of the universe. Basics of Newtonian physics, modeling the universe with simple physical calculations and calculating its age. Interpretation of the shape, beginning and end of the universe with today's modern universe models. The big bang and the formation of the first chemical elements in the universe. Origins of other chemical elements in the universe. Telescopes and spacecrafts that scientifically study the universe.
learning_outcomes: Expresses the range of distances in the universe by using appropriate units.
Describes the basic features of celestial bodies in the universe in a few sentences.
Expresses the main features of the Milky Way Galaxy.
Lists several known properties of dark matter.
Describes the general characteristics of galaxies and galaxy clusters in the universe and explains by which criteria they are classified.
Discusses which cosmological assumptions can be made when looking at the universe on a large scale.
Evaluates a few available evidence of the expansion of the universe.
Relates the concepts of space and time.
Compares the basic properties of simple universe models with each other and creates a critical universe model using basic physics laws.
Describes how the beginning and the end of the universe can be and what phase of the universe we are in at present in the light of modern evolution models.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Units, Distances and Types of Radiation for the Universe
Hafta 4: Milky Way Galaxy and Dark Matter
Hafta 6: Galaxy Clusters and Large-Scale Universe
Hafta 7: The Expansion of the Universe and the Hubble Law
Hafta 9: First Models of the Universe and Dark Energy
Hafta 10: Modeling the universe with Newtonian Physics
Hafta 11: The Future of the Universe According to Modern Universe Models
Hafta 13: The Origin of Chemical Elements
Hafta 14: Telescopes and Spacecrafts that Examine the Deep Universe
resources: “An Introduction to Galaxies and Cosmology (2nd Edition)”, Mark H. Jones, Robert J.A. Lambourne, and Stephen Serjeant, 2015, Cambridge University Press

courses.purpose: The primary objective of this course is to introduce the fundamental concepts of semantics and to teach the principles and methods of meaning analysis.
courses.description: This course examines core issues in lexical and sentential semantics—including the semantic features of nouns and verbs, entailment, presupposition, modality, deixis, thematic roles, reference, context, and inference—through the theoretical frameworks of componential analysis, formal semantics, and cognitive semantics. learning_outcomes: Enhances and further develops the competencies acquired in the field of semantics.
Formulates semantic problem statements based on Turkish language data and offers theoretically grounded solutions.
Develops a theoretical and practical understanding of semantic analysis and utilizes this expertise in the examination of Turkish linguistic data.

Hafta 14: General Evaluation and Current Debates

courses.name: CLIMATE CHANGE EDUCATION AND ENVIRONMENTAL PARADIGMS
courses.purpose: The primary goal of this course is to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of climate science, explain the causes and consequences of climate change based on scientific principles, and develop critical thinking skills on these issues. By the end of the course, students will be able to analyze global and local climate challenges, propose solutions, and evaluate them from a sustainability perspective.
courses.description: The course begins with the definition of climate, its distinction from weather, and the mechanisms behind its formation. It explores the role of solar radiation, energy balance, atmospheric circulation, and ocean currents, along with key components such as temperature, precipitation, humidity, wind, and pressure. Paleoclimate studies and evidence from past geological periods are examined through methods like ice cores, tree rings, and sediment analysis. Natural climate change mechanisms, including Milankovitch cycles, volcanic eruptions, and solar activity, are discussed alongside examples of ice ages and abrupt climate events. Recent climate research is addressed through observational data and climate models, focusing on global warming trends, sea-level rise, and the increase in extreme weather events. The impact of urbanization on climate, the urban heat island effect, and sustainable urban planning are explored. The consequences of global climate change; such as rising sea levels, biodiversity loss, food security challenges, and climate-induced migration; are analyzed, along with regional vulnerabilities. Climate change scenarios specific to Turkey, including drought and desertification risks, impacts on agriculture and water resources, and national policies and strategies, are also covered. Further sections examine international organizations combating climate change, global agreements such as the Paris Agreement and Kyoto Protocol, and the roles of national and local institutions as well as NGOs. Future climate scenarios, emission pathways, and uncertainties in climate models are discussed. Individual actions to reduce carbon footprints, adopt sustainable lifestyles, and promote awareness are emphasized. The concept of the Anthropocene and the extent of human influence (from the Industrial Revolution to present-day ecosystem changes) are analyzed, followed by ethical and philosophical debates on anthropocentrism versus ecocentrism. The course also considers natural climate variability without human influence, drawing on examples from past geological eras, and concludes with posthumanist perspectives on climate change, technological solutions, and the state of climate in a post-human world.
learning_outcomes: Distinguishes between the concepts of climate and weather based on scientific principles, and explains the components, operational dynamics, and interaction processes of the climate system.

Identifies paleo-climate archives (ice cores, tree rings, sediment records, etc.) and interprets past climate conditions using these data; analyzes the mechanisms driving natural climate variability (Milankovitch cycles, volcanism, solar activity) within their historical and geological context.
Critically evaluates observational data sets and climate models used in contemporary climate science, and synthesizes core scientific findings related to global warming.

Hafta 3: Natural climate change mechanisms and geological changes
Hafta 4: Current climate research and key findings on global warming
Hafta 5: Urbanization, urban heat island effect and sustainable urban planning
Hafta 6: Environmental-social impacts and spatial distribution of global climate change
Hafta 7: Climate change scenarios and national strategies in turkey
Hafta 8: National and international actors in the fight against climate change
Hafta 9: Future scenarios climate and RCP projections
Hafta 10: Carbon footprint reduction methods and individual awareness
Hafta 11: The anthropocene concept and humanity's impact on the planet
Hafta 12: Climate ethics in the context of anthropocentrism and ecocentrism
Hafta 13: Natural climate change processes without human factors
Hafta 14: The future of human-nature relationship within the framework of a posthumanist approach

courses.name: PHASED ARRAY RADAR TECHNOLOGIES
courses.purpose: Electronically Scanned Arrays (ETDs) are antennas that provide controllable, agile, high-gain beams for areas such as radar and communications. Unlike antennas that require a reflector or waveguide to steer the array beam, ETDs can steer the beam electronically in space without physical movement of the array. Within the scope of the course, the basic theory of ETDs will be explained and their applications in radar and communication systems will be introduced.
courses.description: Electronic Raster Array Fundamentals; Sub-Array Beam Steering; Beam Optimization; Electronic Raster Array Applications (Radar); Electronic Raster Array Applications (Communication).
learning_outcomes: Masters the basics of Electronic Raster Array.
Can design Electronic Raster Array.
Have knowledge about Electronic Scanning Array Applications (Communication).
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Electronic Interlace Array Fundamentals Lecture 1
Hafta 2: Electronic Raster Array Fundamentals Lecture 2
Hafta 3: Electronic Raster Array Fundamentals Lecture 3
Hafta 4: Electronic Raster Array Fundamentals Lecture 4
Hafta 5: Electronic Raster Array Fundamentals Lecture 5

Hafta 12: Electronic Scanning Array Applications (Radar) Lecture 3
Hafta 13: Electronic Scanning Array Applications (Communication) Lecture 1
Hafta 14: Electronic Scanning Array Applications (Communication) Lecture 2

courses.name: COMPUTER AIDED MODELING OF ENERGY ORIENTED CHEMICAL PROCESSES
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is for the students to have theoretical knowledge about the computer aided modeling of all chemical processes in general and energy-oriented chemical processes in particular, and more importantly, to have an important practice in the aforementioned field thanks to the curriculum that includes many applications and case studies.
courses.description: This course covers the explanation of basic chemical processes and their integration in computer environment simulation studies. In addition, it covers different computer-aided modeling and optimization processes required for a more detailed examination of the simulated processes and the transfer of the models obtained as a result of these processes to the simulation environment.
learning_outcomes: Comprehend the thermodynamic and kinetic concepts of chemical processes in more detail.
Gain knowledge about energy processes.
Have knowledge about simulation tools.
Gain knowledge of the details of basic chemical processes.
Have knowledge about mathematical modeling tools.
Gain substantial experience in integrated chemical process simulation and modeling of basic processes.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: General introduction of energy processes
Hafta 2: General introduction of mathematical modeling and simulation tools
Hafta 5: Case study 1 – Heat exchanger sizing
Hafta 13: Advanced simulation techniques – UDF integration-1
Hafta 14: Case study 3 – Integrated process simulation
resources: Aspen Tutorials
Dominic C.Y. Foo, Chemical Engineering Process Simulation, 2nd ed., Elsevier, 2022
Juma Haydary, Chemical Process Design and Simulation: Aspen Plus and Aspen Hysys Applications, Wiley, 2019.

courses.name: Deep Learning
courses.purpose: Aim of this course is to explain the recent techniques that are used in deep learning. Course also provides students opportunities for reading variety of different articles/papers in the domain; students will also develop deep learning applications via a project during the course time.
courses.description: The course will cover recent research topics in deep learning domain. In the context of this course, first, the fundamentals of deep learning and its historical development will be explained. In this context; loss functions, optimization, shallow neural networks and backpropagation algorithms will be covered in detail. In the second part, Convolutional Neural Networks and Recurrent Neural Networks will be explained and a detailed analysis of the most widely used architectures with best performances in some domains, i.e. with respect to performances, complexity, parameter count, memory requirements etc. will be investigated. In this part, the lectures will also cover the fundamental issues and a detailed presentation of the recently published techniques about training these networks. In the third part, the lectures will focus on the generative networks and the recent methods that use these generative networks will be discussed in detail. In addition, one of the important deep network analysis techniques, i.e. network visualization, will be introduced. Finally, the content will be completed with an introduction to Deep Reinforcement Learning models.
learning_outcomes: Understanding the basic principles of artificial neural networks.
Learning the backpropagation algorithm for graph based neural networks for training.
Deep CNN development capability.
Understanding the working principles of deep generative networks.
Capability of determining the best deep model as a solution for a realistic problem.
Deep RNN development capability.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to deep learning; basic problems and approaches; history
Hafta 5: Training Deep Models 1: Activation Functions, Regularization Techniques
Hafta 6: Training Deep Models 2: Model ensembles, Data augmentation techniques, Transfer learning
Hafta 7: Deep learning frameworks (CPUs, GPUs, PyTorch, Tensorflow, Network graph structures)
Hafta 8: CNN Architectures (AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet)
Hafta 9: CNN Applications (Object detection, Semantic segmentation, Instance segmentation)
Hafta 10: Introduction to Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN, LSTM, GRU)
Hafta 11: Recurrent Neural Networks Applications (Language modeling, Image description, Attention modeling)
Hafta 12: Generative Models ( Pixel RNN/RCNN, Variational Autoencoders, GANs)
resources: Chollet, Francois. Deep Learning with Python. Simon and Schuster, 2021.
Goodfellow, Ian, Yoshua Bengio, and Aaron Courville. Deep learning. MIT press, 2016.
